items.myth: Gradient descent always finds the global minimum.
items.reality: GD follows local slope information; on non-convex loss surfaces it may stop at local minima, saddle regions or plateaus without reaching a global optimum.
items.example: L(w) = w⁴ − 3w² + w has multiple critical points; GD from different starts can land at different local minima.

items.myth: Batch gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent are unrelated algorithms.
items.reality: They share the same update rule θ ← θ − η∇L(θ); SGD differs only in how ∇L is estimated (subset vs full dataset).
items.example: PyTorch `SGD` with batch size equal to dataset size implements batch GD; batch size 64 implements mini-batch SGD.

items.myth: If loss stops decreasing, the model has converged to the best solution.
items.reality: Plateaus, saddle points, vanishing gradients and too-small η can stall progress far from a useful minimum.
items.example: Saturated sigmoid units can yield tiny ∇L; loss is flat while predictions remain poor until initialization or architecture changes.

items.myth: Larger learning rate always speeds up training.
items.reality: η too large overshoots minima and can diverge; batch GD's smooth loss curve still oscillates or blows up with excessive η.
items.example: On L(w) = w² starting at w = 10, η = 1.1 alternates sign and diverges even though ∇L is exact each step.
